content.text: Every large language model falls into one of two camps based on a single question: can you download and run the model's weights yourself? Open-weight models publish those weights so anyone can host, fine-tune, and audit them. Closed models keep the weights private and grant access only through an HTTP API. For agentic AI systems — which may issue thousands of LLM calls per day and process sensitive documents — this choice cascades into cost structure, data governance, latency, vendor dependency, and long-term reproducibility all at once.

(often loosely called open-source) publishes the numerical parameters that define the network — the billions of floating-point values learned during training. Recipients can download them, run inference on their own hardware, modify them through fine-tuning, and redistribute derivatives. Notable examples include Meta's Llama 3.1 (7B, 70B, 405B), Mistral / Mixtral MoE, Qwen 2.5, DeepSeek-R1, Phi-3, and Gemma 2. Licenses vary — Apache 2.0, MIT, and custom community licenses — and must be reviewed before commercial deployment.

keeps those parameters secret and provides access exclusively through a managed API. The provider operates the infrastructure, bills per token, and controls the model version. Examples include GPT-4o and o1 (OpenAI), Claude 3.5 / 3.7 Sonnet (Anthropic), Gemini 1.5 Pro and Ultra (Google), and Command R+ (Cohere). The term 'open-source' in the strict OSI sense additionally requires that training code and data be public — which almost no frontier model satisfies — so 'open-weight' is the more accurate term used throughout this document.

Agentic systems differ from one-shot question-answering in ways that amplify the open-vs-closed decision. An agent orchestrates multi-step reasoning loops — often 10–100 sequential LLM calls to complete one task. If each call costs fractions of a cent in API tokens, a high-volume production pipeline generating one billion tokens per month can face a five-figure monthly API bill. At that scale, self-hosted GPU compute amortizes to a fraction of per-token pricing.

Data governance is the second major amplifier. Agentic systems commonly operate over sensitive material: legal contracts, patient records, financial filings, proprietary source code. Sending this data to a closed API means it traverses the provider's network and is processed on the provider's hardware, regardless of enterprise DPA agreements. Regulated industries under HIPAA, SOX, or GDPR often cannot accept this. Running open weights on controlled infrastructure eliminates the data-egress problem by definition.

A third factor is reproducibility. Closed providers silently update models. Prompts engineered for one version may degrade after a silent backend update to the same endpoint name. Open model weights are immutable once downloaded — the weights you serve today are exactly those weights in five years, enabling reproducible evaluations and regression testing for regulated or scientific applications.

Think of a closed model as ordering from a high-end restaurant. The food arrives expert-prepared; the chef's recipe and technique are invisible. You pay per dish, you cannot substitute ingredients, and you are entirely dependent on the restaurant staying open. An open model is buying raw ingredients from a grocery store. You need a kitchen (GPU infrastructure) and cooking knowledge (ML engineering), but you have complete control: inspect everything, customize freely, and replicate the meal exactly for years. Many production systems use both — the restaurant for complex, high-stakes dishes and the grocery store for high-volume everyday cooking.

Hybrid strategy: route by task type. Use closed models for tasks where quality is paramount and data is not sensitive. Use open models for high-volume or privacy-constrained subtasks. A single abstract LLM interface (LiteLLM, LangChain) lets the agent code remain unchanged while the router swaps backends.
Weights run inside your own VPC or on-premise. Patient records, legal documents, and trade secrets never leave the perimeter — a binary requirement for many regulated industries.
GPU compute has a fixed marginal cost. A fine-tuned Llama 3.1 70B on four A100s costs roughly $8/hr regardless of token volume. At high throughput this is 10–100× cheaper than API pricing.

Modify the weights directly via supervised fine-tuning (SFT), LoRA adapters, or DPO to specialize the model for a narrow domain. A legal-clause classifier fine-tuned on your case law substantially outperforms a general-purpose model on that task.
Your application is not coupled to a provider's pricing decisions, deprecation cycles, or usage-policy enforcement. The weights are yours indefinitely.
Co-locating the model with your compute eliminates external network round-trips — critical for agentic loops requiring 20–50 sequential calls to complete in seconds.
The frontier of reasoning, coding, and multimodal understanding has historically been held by closed models. The gap with open models is narrowing but persists for the hardest multi-step tasks.
No GPU procurement, no inference serving stack, no model updates. Send an HTTP request and receive a result — small teams build powerful agents with no ML engineering overhead.
Providers operate redundant infrastructure with SLAs, automatic failover, and autoscaling. Matching this uptime with a self-hosted cluster is significant work.
When a provider ships vision support, extended context, or new tool-use schemas, you gain access immediately via API without redeployment.

Self-hosting a 70B model requires 4–8 high-end GPUs (~$10k–40k hardware or $8–16/hr cloud), an inference serving stack (vLLM, TGI, Triton), autoscaling logic, and on-call engineering. This operational burden is not trivial.

The best open models (Llama 3.1 405B, DeepSeek-R1) are competitive with mid-tier closed models but can lag behind the strongest closed models on complex multi-step reasoning. When model quality directly drives business value, this gap matters.
Open models do not carry the same depth of safety tuning. If your application must refuse harmful requests, you must implement guardrails yourself (NeMo Guardrails, Llama Guard, custom classifiers).

'Open' does not mean 'do anything.' Meta's Llama 3 license restricts use by companies above 700 million monthly active users and prohibits training competing foundation models. Always audit the license before production deployment.
At one billion tokens per month, GPT-4o at $2.50/M input tokens costs $2,500 for input alone, scaling linearly with no volume discount path.
Even with enterprise DPA agreements, data is processed on the provider's hardware. This is a binary disqualifier for many regulated applications.
Silent model updates create regression risk.
Providers update models without announcement. Prompts tuned for one version may produce different or degraded outputs after an unannounced update.
API rate limits can throttle high-throughput agentic workloads. Provider outages immediately halt your entire application.
When running an open model locally, the weight tensor is loaded from disk into GPU VRAM. VRAM requirement scales with parameter count and quantization precision:

A 7B model at 4-bit quantization requires ~3.5 GB (consumer GPU territory), while a 70B model at 4-bit needs ~35 GB (two A100-40GB or one A100-80GB). During inference, each autoregressive step performs a forward pass through the transformer at a cost of roughly
is the parameter count. Modern inference engines like vLLM use
and continuous batching to serve multiple concurrent requests from a shared KV cache, maximizing GPU utilization under load.

With a closed model, your application makes an HTTPS POST request to the provider's endpoint. The request body contains the model ID, messages array, and generation parameters as JSON. The provider routes the request to available hardware, runs inference, and returns the completion (streaming or batch). Every call is a network round trip: typical time-to-first-token (TTFT) is 150–600 ms depending on provider queue depth. For agentic loops with 30 sequential calls, this accumulates to 4.5–18 seconds of pure network wait, ignoring generation time.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram comparing open-weight and closed/proprietary model access patterns in an agentic AI application

content.text: The diagram shows both model families feeding the same agentic application through an abstract interface layer. Open-weight models connect via a local process call or self-hosted HTTP endpoint (green); closed models connect exclusively through the provider's HTTPS API (blue). An abstraction library such as LiteLLM or LangChain's chat-model interface sits between the agent orchestrator and both backends, so routing decisions reduce to a configuration string and a privacy classifier — not a code change.
Your data is regulated, sensitive, or contractually prohibited from leaving your infrastructure (healthcare under HIPAA, finance under SOX, EU companies under GDPR).
Usage volume is high enough that per-token API costs exceed GPU infrastructure — typically above 500 million tokens per month for a 70B-class model.
You need domain-specific fine-tuning: the general model does not meet your quality bar and labeled training data is available.
You need strict version pinning for reproducibility — regulatory audits, clinical trials, or long-running A/B tests where the model must be held constant.
Inference latency inside an agentic loop is a bottleneck and co-location with compute would materially reduce it.
Absolute best capability on complex, novel reasoning is the primary constraint and data sensitivity is not a concern.
Your team lacks ML infrastructure expertise and the overhead of managing GPU clusters is not justified by current usage volume.
You are building a prototype or early-stage product where speed to market beats cost optimization.
You need multimodal capabilities (vision, audio, video) that open models do not yet cover with comparable quality.

Many production agentic systems avoid an exclusive choice. A document pipeline might use a closed model to extract the core question from a complex legal filing, then route summarization of 50 supporting documents through a self-hosted open model. The hybrid approach captures peak quality for rare high-stakes steps while controlling cost and data exposure for the high-volume bulk.

The fastest path to model-agnosticism is LiteLLM, which normalizes calls to 100+ providers — including self-hosted vLLM endpoints — behind the OpenAI completion signature. Install dependencies and configure credentials:

pip install litellm vllm anthropic

# Closed-model credentials
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

# Launch a local open model (requires GPU with sufficient VRAM)
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
    --port 8000 \
    --max-model-len 8192

# Verify
curl http://localhost:8000/health

With vLLM running locally, both backends share the same calling convention through LiteLLM. The only difference between a closed and an open call is the model string and, for a local endpoint, the api_base parameter:

import litellm

def call_model(messages: list[dict], use_open: bool = False) -> str:
    """Unified completion call routing to either backend."""
    if use_open:
        response = litellm.completion(
            model="openai/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
            messages=messages,
            api_base="http://localhost:8000/v1",
            api_key="none",     # vLLM requires a key field but ignores the value
            max_tokens=512,
        )
    else:
        response = litellm.completion(
            model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",   # always use a pinned version
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=512,
        )
    return response.choices[0].message.content


messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain paged attention in two sentences."}]
print(call_model(messages, use_open=False))   # closed
print(call_model(messages, use_open=True))    # open

"""
Benchmark time-to-first-token and throughput for a closed vs open model.
Requires vLLM running at localhost:8000 and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set.
"""
import time
import litellm

PROMPT = "Summarize the key tradeoffs between mutex and semaphore in concurrency."

def benchmark(model: str, api_base: str | None = None, n: int = 5) -> dict:
    times = []
    for _ in range(n):
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        r = litellm.completion(
            model=model,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
            api_base=api_base,
            api_key="none" if api_base else None,
            max_tokens=200,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
        times.append(elapsed)
    return {
        "model": model,
        "avg_s": round(sum(times) / len(times), 3),
        "min_s": round(min(times), 3),
    }


closed = benchmark("claude-3-5-haiku-20241022")
open_  = benchmark(
    "openai/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    api_base="http://localhost:8000/v1",
)

for r in (closed, open_):
    print(f"{r['model']:50s}  avg={r['avg_s']}s  min={r['min_s']}s")
Example 2 — privacy-aware router for an agentic pipeline

"""
Route tasks to an open model when they contain sensitive content,
and to a closed model otherwise, using a unified LiteLLM interface.
"""
from __future__ import annotations
import re
import litellm

SENSITIVE = re.compile(
    r"\b(SSN|social.security|credit.card|patient|diagnosis|"
    r"salary|confidential|proprietary|internal.only)\b",
    re.IGNORECASE,
)

CLOSED_MODEL = "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
OPEN_MODEL   = "openai/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct"
OPEN_BASE    = "http://inference.internal:8000/v1"


def is_sensitive(text: str) -> bool:
    return bool(SENSITIVE.search(text))


def agent_call(user_message: str) -> dict:
    """Route and execute a single agent step."""
    sensitive = is_sensitive(user_message)
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    if sensitive:
        resp = litellm.completion(
            model=OPEN_MODEL,
            messages=messages,
            api_base=OPEN_BASE,
            api_key="none",
            max_tokens=512,
        )
        backend = "open"
    else:
        resp = litellm.completion(
            model=CLOSED_MODEL,
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=512,
        )
        backend = "closed"

    return {
        "backend": backend,
        "sensitive": sensitive,
        "text": resp.choices[0].message.content,
    }


# Usage
result_a = agent_call("What is the capital of France?")
result_b = agent_call("Summarize this patient diagnosis: hypertension, T2 diabetes.")

print(f"Query A → backend={result_a['backend']}")   # closed
print(f"Query B → backend={result_b['backend']}")   # open

A mid-size law firm builds an agentic contract review system. The system receives uploaded PDFs, extracts clauses, flags risk terms, and drafts summaries for lawyer review. Monthly volume: 50,000 contracts generating roughly 1 billion input tokens and 100 million output tokens across agent steps. All documents contain confidential client information — sending them to a third-party API raises GDPR and professional responsibility concerns.

The production architecture uses a hybrid model. A fine-tuned Llama 3.1 70B runs on four leased A100-80GB GPUs ($3.50/hr each, $10,080/month fixed). It handles clause extraction, risk flagging, and summarization — the bulk of token volume. Claude 3.5 Sonnet is called only for clauses the local model flags with confidence below 0.75 (about 3% of all clauses), at a monthly API cost of roughly $135. Total monthly inference cost: ~$10,215 vs. ~$4,500 API-only for a 70B open model — but the open model also eliminates all data-egress risk and satisfies regulatory review.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import json, litellm

@dataclass
class ClauseResult:
    risk_level: str      # low / medium / high
    confidence: float
    summary: str
    model_used: str

OPEN_BASE = "http://inference.internal:8000/v1"

def review_clause(clause_text: str) -> ClauseResult:
    # First pass: local fine-tuned model (fast, private)
    local_resp = litellm.completion(
        model="openai/LlamaLegal-70B",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f'Analyze this contract clause. Respond with JSON '
            f'{{"risk_level":"low|medium|high","confidence":0.0-1.0,"summary":"..."}}\n{clause_text}'}],
        api_base=OPEN_BASE, api_key="none",
        max_tokens=256, response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    )
    parsed = json.loads(local_resp.choices[0].message.content)

    if parsed["confidence"] >= 0.75:
        return ClauseResult(
            risk_level=parsed["risk_level"],
            confidence=parsed["confidence"],
            summary=parsed["summary"],
            model_used="open",
        )

    # Escalate low-confidence clauses to the closed frontier model
    closed_resp = litellm.completion(
        model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Analyze this contract clause for legal risk:\n{clause_text}"}],
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    return ClauseResult(
        risk_level="high",          # conservative on escalations
        confidence=1.0,
        summary=closed_resp.choices[0].message.content,
        model_used="closed",
    )

Closed providers update the model behind an endpoint without versioning the URL. Using a bare model name instead of a dated version string silently changes behavior after an unannounced update. Always pin:
. For open models, record the exact Hugging Face commit SHA or model card version in your deployment manifest.

Many developers download Llama weights and deploy commercially without reading the Meta Llama license. The license permits commercial use but restricts companies above 700 million MAU and prohibits using Llama outputs to train competing foundation models. Mistral 7B and Falcon 40B carry Apache 2.0, which is genuinely permissive. Always verify the license during a model adoption review before any production deployment.
Loading a 70B model from disk into GPU memory takes 60–120 seconds. Kubernetes HPAs that set
will cold-start on the first request after scale-down, causing timeouts. Set
to maintain at least one warm replica, and configure a keep-alive ping if your serving infra still scales the pod down during idle periods.

Tightly coupling prompt construction to one provider's API shape (Anthropic's system parameter placement, Google's parts array, OpenAI's tool-call format) creates migration friction when switching or A/B testing backends. Use an abstraction layer from day one — LiteLLM handles format normalization automatically across all major providers.
Design for model-agnosticism from the start.
Use LiteLLM or a thin wrapper so model selection is a config value. This allows A/B testing and migration without refactoring agent code.
In production, use dated version strings for closed models and recorded commit SHAs for open-model deployments. Never let model behavior drift silently.
Build an evaluation harness before picking a model.
Run representative production tasks through candidate models and measure accuracy, latency, and cost. General benchmark rankings (MMLU, HumanEval) often do not predict domain-specific performance.
Start closed, migrate to open at scale.
Closed models eliminate infrastructure overhead during prototyping. Once the application is proven and volume grows, evaluate whether a fine-tuned open model is cost-effective for high-volume tasks.
Have the open model produce a structured confidence score. Route low-confidence outputs to a more capable closed model rather than serving uncertain results.
Test failure modes for both types.
For closed models: implement exponential-backoff retry and fallback to an alternate provider on 429 or 5xx. For open models: handle VRAM OOM errors and host failures with circuit breakers.

A single A100-80GB running vLLM with Llama 3.1 70B (4-bit quantized, ~35 GB) sustains roughly 500–800 output tokens per second under continuous batching. A 4-GPU node reaches 2,000–3,000 tokens/s. Compare this to closed API limits typically set at 100,000–800,000 tokens per minute (1,600–13,000 tokens/s) shared across all requests. Latency inside an agentic loop is dominated by time-to-first-token (TTFT):

For co-located inference, TTFT on a 1,024-token input with a 70B model is typically 80–200 ms — often faster than a closed API (150–600 ms) because the external network round-trip is eliminated. Quantization trades quality for memory and throughput:
— highest-throughput production serving; OpenAI-compatible API; supports paged attention, continuous batching, speculative decoding.
— Hugging Face's server; strong integration with HF Hub; tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs.
— CPU and consumer GPU inference via GGUF quantization; zero infrastructure overhead; ideal for development or lightweight on-device deployment.
— unified Python SDK and proxy for 100+ providers; normalizes message formats, retries, and logging.
— agent frameworks with built-in chat-model abstractions that swap providers at instantiation.
— observability and routing gateways that add caching, fallback logic, and cost tracking as a sidecar.

# Install fine-tuning stack
pip install transformers datasets peft trl bitsandbytes

# LoRA fine-tuning example with TRL's SFTTrainer
python - <<'EOF'
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig
import torch

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    quantization_config=BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True),
    device_map="auto",
)

lora_cfg = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=["q_proj","v_proj"])
dataset   = load_dataset("json", data_files="train.jsonl", split="train")

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=SFTConfig(output_dir="./lora-output", num_train_epochs=3,
                   per_device_train_batch_size=4),
    train_dataset=dataset,
    peft_config=lora_cfg,
)
trainer.train()
EOF
The table below compares the two primary access strategies along dimensions that matter most for production agentic systems:

Every API call transmits input data to the provider's infrastructure. Even with enterprise DPA and SOC 2 certification, this is a non-zero exfiltration surface for prompt injection attacks targeting the model endpoint.
You trust the provider's model not to leak data between requests, not to be backdoored, and to accurately represent the model version behind the endpoint.
A leaked API key grants unrestricted access and billing liability. Rotate keys, use environment-specific secrets, and set per-key spending limits.
Weights downloaded from unofficial sources may be backdoored. Always verify SHA256 checksums against the official Hugging Face model card and use only verified model IDs from trusted organizations.
Open base models have less safety tuning than fine-tuned closed models. Wrap the inference endpoint with Llama Guard or NeMo Guardrails to filter harmful outputs for customer-facing applications.
An unprotected vLLM endpoint on a network-accessible port is a security liability. Always place the server behind a VPC, require authentication, and restrict access to internal services only.
Open-weight models publish their parameters for download, self-hosting, fine-tuning, and redistribution; closed models grant access only through a managed API with hidden weights.
Data governance is the most common forcing function: regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) often cannot send data to third-party APIs and must self-host open models.
Cost economics favor open models above roughly 500 million tokens per month — below that threshold, the zero-infrastructure overhead of a closed API typically wins.
Open models require you to own the infrastructure stack: GPU provisioning, inference serving (vLLM, TGI), autoscaling, alignment guardrails, and on-call operations.
Pin model versions in both strategies: dated version strings for closed APIs, weight commit SHAs for open deployments. Silent updates are a production reliability risk in both camps.
Use an abstraction layer (LiteLLM, LangChain chat models) from day one so the agent code remains unchanged when you swap or A/B test backends.

The strongest production pattern is a hybrid router: closed models for high-stakes, low-volume reasoning steps; open models for high-volume or privacy-constrained subtasks, gated by a confidence threshold or sensitivity classifier.
